The xhr2 library has a real security flaw in SSR, the fetch migration is three lines, and you have one major version to get it done.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Three days ago, the Angular 22.0.4 patch landed with a changelog note easy to scroll past: XHR support in @angular/platform-server is deprecated, use standard fetch APIs instead. Most teams will treat that as noise. They should not.
This is not a “we prefer the newer API” deprecation. The official Angular HTTP setup docs spell out the reason: the underlying xhr2 library does not safely handle redirects. Specifically, it can forward Authorization headers on cross-origin redirects and is susceptible to denial-of-service via redirect loops. If your Angular Universal or SSR app is still going through XHR on the server, those risks are shipping to production with every request cycle.
Angular 23 removes the server-side XHR backend entirely. You have one major version window to migrate.
Why the Common Shortcut Bites Later
The typical path to this problem is withFetch(). Before Angular 22, you opted into the Fetch backend explicitly. Teams that never added withFetch() to their provideHttpClient() call stayed on XHR, and ng update did not remove that choice for them. It added…
